rows.v: Macadamia, Pecan, Cashew, Pine Nut
High in fat — best toasted lightly to deepen their natural richness.
Pine nuts are the edible seeds tucked inside pine cones — harvested mostly by hand, which is why they're one of the priciest nuts in the kitchen.
Almond, Hazelnut, Pistachio
Baking and snacking staples — equally at home raw, roasted, or ground into flour or butter.
Almonds — like peaches and cherries — are the seed inside a drupe, so botanically the almond is the pit, not a true nut.
Walnut, Brazil Nut, Chestnut
Strong, distinctive flavors — best in small amounts or paired with something sweet.
A couple of Brazil nuts can cover a full day's selenium — they're one of the richest natural sources on earth, so a small handful is plenty.
Peanut, Pumpkin Seed (Pepita), Sunflower Seed, Sesame, Chia Seed, Flaxseed, Hemp Seed
Nutrient-dense additions to almost any dish — sprinkle on salads, blend into spreads, or snack as-is.
The peanut isn't a nut at all — it's a legume that flowers above ground then ripens its pods underground, closer kin to peas and beans than to almonds.
Strawberry, Blueberry, Raspberry, Blackberry
Best fresh and in season — also freeze well for smoothies and baking year-round.
Botanically the strawberry isn't a berry — its seeds sit on the outside, making it an 'accessory fruit,' while the humble blueberry is a true berry.
Cranberry, Gooseberry, Black Currant, Boysenberry
Too tart to eat raw in quantity — shine in jams, sauces, and baked goods with added sugar.
Ripe cranberries bounce — growers use a bounce test, and tiny air pockets let the berries float, which is why bogs are flooded at harvest.
Mulberry, Elderberry, Saskatoon Berry (Juneberry), Cloudberry, Salmonberry
Less common in stores — look for them at farmers markets or in jams and preserves.
Raw elderberries are mildly toxic and must be cooked before eating — one of the few berries that's a syrup-and-jam fruit rather than an out-of-hand snack.
Goji Berry, Açaí, Bilberry
Prized for antioxidant content — often sold dried, frozen, or as juice/powder rather than fresh.
Açaí is technically a drupe like an olive — almost all pit, with only a thin skin and pulp, which is why it's sold as frozen purée rather than fresh fruit.
